title: Mexican Guacamole

subs.name: not really substitutable, but mashed boiled peas with lemon and mint makes an interesting green dip in a pinch
raw mango juice adds a similar tartness and is very Indian-kitchen friendly

ingredients.name: white or red onion, finely diced

subs.name: a pinch of red chilli powder for heat without the crunch

steps.text: Halve the avocados, remove the stones and scoop the flesh into a bowl. Add the lime juice and a good pinch of salt immediately — the acid prevents browning.

Mash the avocado with a fork to your preferred texture — some people want it completely smooth, most prefer it chunky with visible pieces. Do not use a blender; hand-mashing gives a far better result.
Fold in the diced onion, tomato, green chilli, coriander and garlic (if using). Taste and adjust lime juice and salt — guacamole should be bright and tangy, not flat.

Serve within 30 minutes for the best colour and flavour. If you need to hold it longer, press a piece of cling film directly onto the surface of the guacamole and refrigerate — the direct contact prevents air browning.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: avocado → not really substitutable, but mashed boiled peas with lemon and mint makes an interesting green dip in a pinch; lemon → raw mango juice adds a similar tartness and is very Indian-kitchen friendly; green-chilli → a pinch of red chilli powder for heat without the crunch
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Avocados are now available in most metro Indian supermarkets and on quick-commerce apps. Look for Hass avocados that yield slightly when pressed — rock-hard ones need 2–3 days at room temperature to ripen. Lime juice from Indian nimbu is a perfect substitute and often preferable for its sharpness. The dip pairs brilliantly with Indian nachos or even as a spread on toast.
